rows.supporting_quote: CHFA supported Salida Ridge Apartments with a reservation of $1,220,000 in federal 9 percent Housing Tax Credits in 2020 and $1,611,000 in permanent financing in 2021. Salida Ridge Apartments provides 48 affordable rental housing units to residents of Salida with incomes between 30 to 50 percent of the Area Median Income. Developed by Commonwealth Development Corporation, Salida Ridge offers a mix of one-, two-, and three-bedroom apartment homes.
rows.notes: Supports: $1.22M HTC 2020, $1.611M permanent financing 2021, 48 units, Commonwealth Development, bedroom mix (1-2-3BR). Does NOT contain: 'Confluent Park' marketing name, 24/21/3 bedroom breakdown, $13M cost, 'first in 20+ years', summer 2022 completion, June 2022 leasing. CHFA states 30-50% AMI, not 30-60% as the brief claims.

rows.supporting_quote: Confluent Park Apartments, a new 48-unit affordable housing unit, has been approved by the City of Salida... This project is being overseen by Commonwealth Development. The project developer was awarded Low Income Housing Tax Credits (LIHTC) from the Colorado Housing Finance Authority (CHFA) to finance the deal. It will be the first LIHTC development in Salida in more than 20 years.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; content verified via WebSearch snippet extraction. Supports: 48 units, Confluent Park branding, Commonwealth Development, LIHTC, 'first LIHTC development in Salida in more than 20 years.' Article is dated May 27, 2020 and predates construction milestones — does NOT cover summer 2022 completion, June 2022 leasing, $13M cost, or 24/21/3 bedroom breakdown.

rows.supporting_quote: The 2024 Round Two announcement lists 10 awarded developments located in 'Aurora, Colorado Springs, Denver, Fort Collins, Glenwood Springs, Granby, Pueblo, and Westminster.' Salida is not mentioned among the locations or project names listed.

The article lists 12 developments awarded Housing Tax Credits in 2025 Round One, located in 'Alamosa, Aurora, Brighton, Denver, Fort Collins, Frisco, Superior, Trinidad, Westminster, and Wheat Ridge.' Salida is not mentioned among these locations or in the detailed list of awarded projects.

rows.notes: Government landing page that links to the underlying round-by-round award reports (2024 R1, R2; 2025 R1, R2) rather than directly stating which jurisdictions did/did not receive awards. Corroborated by the per-round announcement pages (s3, s4, and independently verified 2024 R1 = no Salida [Saguache, Boulder, Battlement Mesa, Denver, etc.] and 2025 R2 = no Salida [Aurora, Denver, Fort Collins, Longmont, Sterling]) but the awards-index page itself does not contain the supporting fact.

rows.supporting_quote: Salida Ballot Issue 2A — Increases the annual short term rental license to $1,000 for affordable housing · For 55% | Against 45%. Salida Ballot Issue 2B — Increases lodging tax on short term rentals from 4.82 per day to $15 for affordable housing · For 51% | Against 47%.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports: both measures passed, 2A = $1,000 annual STR license, 2B raised OLT to $15/day. Source labels its numbers PRELIMINARY (Nov 9, 2022); the specific final counts in the brief (2A 1,843-1,512; 2B 1,702-1,647) are not on this page — the percentages here translate to a smaller vote universe than the brief's totals. Also: this source reports the previous OLT as $4.82/day, not $3.66/day as the brief states; the $3.66 figure exists in other contemporaneous Salida sources but is not on this page.

rows.supporting_quote: In 2022, voters in Salida supported raising the tax visitors pay when they stay at a short-term rental in their communities. Measure 2A increased the annual short term rental license to $1,000 for affordable housing (passing 55% to 45%), while Measure 2B increased the lodging tax on short term rentals from $4.82 per day to $15 for affordable housing (passing 51% to 47%). If both measures were approved, they would generate an estimated $800,000, which would likely be used to leverage additional grant money for housing.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports: both measures passed in Nov 2022, $1,000 STR license, $15/day OLT, ~$800K combined estimated revenue, use for affordable housing leveraged with state grants. Source reports prior OLT as $4.82/day (not $3.66/day as the brief claims) and reports percentages (55/45, 51/47) consistent with the brief's final counts (1,843-1,512 ≈ 55/45; 1,702-1,647 ≈ 51/49) but does not quote those vote totals verbatim.

WebFetch direct access denied by harness. Via WebSearch corroboration: a separate Ark Valley Voice piece states 'In Salida, short-term rental lodging taxes go towards affordable housing measures and code restrictions (§6-6-20) limit the number of short-term rentals in residential areas to one per block face' and the city's own pages confirm 'The City has contributed $1.25 million from its Housing Fund, which is supported by short term rental tax revenues.' The block-face cap is in Salida Municipal Code §6-6-20 and is summarized on this commdev STR landing page, but I could not retrieve and quote verbatim. Treating as partial: the page is the policy hub but I cannot independently confirm verbatim wording.

rows.supporting_quote: After a few weeks of community input requesting an amendment to the fees in lieu of the 2024 fee schedule, Salida City Council voted 4-1 to reduce fees from a flat rate of $17.40 per square foot during the May 7 regular meeting... The motion passed, with Fontana dissenting.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports: May 2024, 4-1 council vote, reduction from $17.40/sq ft flat rate. Article does NOT speak to the brief's claim that the Inclusionary Housing Ordinance was adopted in 2018 nor that it was amended via Ordinance No. 10, Series of 2021 — those details are corroborated by other public sources (Mountain Mail Ordinance No. 10 Series of 2021 legal notice) but not in this specific article.

rows.supporting_quote: On February 17, 2026, Council approved to amend Chapter 6 of Article VI of the Salida Municipal Code regarding short-term rental licenses within the City of Salida, and these regulations are effective as of March 23, 2026.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction of the city clerk page content. Supports the brief's exact dates (Feb 17, 2026 council approval; Mar 23, 2026 effective).

rows.supporting_quote: The City of Salida purchased the 190 acre Vandaveer Ranch near the south east corner of Salida in 2004 in order to obtain its water rights. The $3.2 million dollar price tag for the ranch included the 192 acres – and all-important water rights on the Tennessee Ditch. In 2009, the Salida Natural Resource Center Development Corporation (NRCDC), a Colorado non-profit corporation, was formed to enable financing for the construction of the Forest Service building on the Vandaveer Ranch without incurring taxpayer liability.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports 2004 purchase, 190 acres, $3.2M, Tennessee Ditch water rights, 2009 NRCDC formation as Colorado nonprofit. Note: article describes NRCDC as 'Natural Resource CENTER Development Corporation' (matches brief). Source also notes the ranch was reconveyed to the city later; the brief's framing 'ranch was deeded to it for financing-structure purposes' aligns with the source's 'enable financing... without incurring taxpayer liability.'

rows.supporting_quote: The City of Salida announced the kickoff of the master planning process for the South Ark Neighborhood—an approximately 100-acre portion of the former Vandaveer Ranch property that is currently owned by the City in January 2023... In 2023, City staff and consultants created and received City Council approval for the South Ark Neighborhood Master Plan.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; content extracted via WebSearch. Supports: ~100 acres city-owned, 2023 Council adoption of master plan. This is the City landing page for the plan; it materially summarizes adoption but does not directly contain the brief's earlier-history facts (2004 purchase, NRCDC). For those, s11 carries the load.

rows.supporting_quote: The preferred master plan includes 400 units, as well as a recreational building on the east side of the property and a ¾ acre park located within the residential area... 300, or 75 percent of units, would need to be deed-restricted, with 200, or 50 percent of units, needing to be in the 'affordable' category, and deed-restricted for a minimum of five years.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports the brief's 400-unit / 300 (75%) deed-restricted / 200 (50%) affordable / 5-year minimum deed-restriction numbers verbatim.

rows.supporting_quote: Two parcels in the middle of the Vandaveer Ranch project, designed tracts 12 and 13 totaling 10.6 acres, are designated for affordable housing.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch. Supports the Tract 12 / Tract 13 / 10.6 acres / 'middle' / affordable housing designation verbatim.

rows.supporting_quote: The City of Salida celebrated the start of construction for the South Ark Neighborhood Phase 1 Infrastructure Project with a groundbreaking ceremony on Thursday, November 13 at 3:00 p.m... The City of Salida has received two, $2 million grants from the state's Strong Communities and More Housing Now initiatives, totaling $4 million, specifically to support the South Ark Neighborhood Infrastructure Phase I project.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction of the city press release. Supports verbatim: Nov 13 (2025) groundbreaking, two $2M state grants ($4M total) from Strong Communities + More Housing Now.

rows.supporting_quote: Resolution 2019-61 approved the Development Plan for 6906 Vandaveer Ranch Road. The roughly two-acre pocket neighborhood would allow for a 32 rental unit development. Four of the 32 units must adhere to the city's inclusionary housing provisions and are required to be maintained as affordable housing.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; PDF content verified via WebSearch extraction of city ordinance pages. Supports: Resolution 2019-61 approves the development plan, 32 units, 4 affordable units. The PDF itself is the development plan resolution; it does not contain the brief's claim about the 14 1-BR + 18 2-BR breakdown, the 'Magpie' marketing name, the 2021 build year, or the parallel Resolution 2019-46 annexation date (Sept 17, 2019) — those details exist in companion Ark Valley Voice coverage of the same project.

rows.supporting_quote: The Chaffee Housing Authority (CHA) is an independent public organization, formed in October 2020 through a joint agreement between Chaffee County, the City of Salida, and the Town of Buena Vista. The Housing Authority Board, governing the work of the CHA, was initially seated in February, 2021.
rows.notes: Verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports formation date (October 2020), the three-party IGA, and Feb 2021 board seating.

rows.supporting_quote: The Chaffee Housing Authority (CHA) is the manager for the City of Salida's Inclusionary Housing Program (IHP)... City of Salida IHP homes are priced for different income levels (up to 140% Area Median Income) and built to accommodate different household sizes (0-3 bedroom).
rows.notes: Verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports: CHA manages the Salida IHP and homes priced up to 140% AMI.

rows.supporting_quote: Chaffee Housing Trust was incorporated in 2007 as Chaffee County Community Land Trust... The Chaffee Housing Trust holds land in perpetuity and uses a 99-year ground lease to keep homes affordable while allowing sellers to realize a limited share of market appreciation... The Chaffee Housing Trust reported 42 homes completed to date, according to a report from October 2025. The trust currently has about 30 homes in development, including 12 units at a railyard site, 14 units in Buena Vista and a planned acquisition of a fourplex in Poncha Springs.
rows.notes: Verified via WebSearch (CHT website content). Supports 2007 founding, Community Land Trust model, 99-year ground lease, shared-equity, 42 completed homes, ~30 in development. CHT's 2007 incorporation was as 'Chaffee County Community Land Trust' (renamed); brief's '2007 founding' aligns.

rows.supporting_quote: Chaffee Housing Authority Ballot Issue 6A: Tax Revenue for Housing Authority – Failed. Chaffee Housing Authority Ballot Issue 6B: Lift TABOR Limits on Revenue for Housing Authority – Passed. The CHA advanced the measure saying it could generate $3.5 million in yearly revenue for the Housing Authority through 2035, with a 'sunset' clause that would require the tax to be re-approved after 12 years. Passing 6B will authorize CHA to retain nearly $3 million in grant funding awarded for the construction of Jane's Place...
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction (this AVV story plus the Chaffee County Times companion piece on the same race). Supports: 6A = 0.5% sales tax, ~$3.5M/yr, 12-year sunset, FAILED; 6B = de-Brucing/TABOR lift, PASSED, enables Jane's Place funding.

rows.supporting_quote: Chaffee County voters rejected ballot measure 1A, with 58.19% voting against the proposed lodging tax increase. Ballot question 1A would have increased the county lodging tax from 1.9% to 5.9% for all lodging establishments, including short-term rentals, hotels and motels.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports verbatim: 58.19% no vote, 1.9% to 5.9% rate change, Nov 2025 (implicit).

rows.supporting_quote: Jane's Place is a development consisting of four buildings that will deliver 17 transitional housing units, including space for those requiring emergency sheltering... Jane's Place is anticipated to welcome its first tenants on January 1, 2026... The Colorado State Housing Board recently awarded $1,287,000 to the Chaffee Housing Authority from the Transformational Affordable Housing Grant Program for Jane's Place. Jane's Place is a unique, mixed-use development planned for a currently vacant half-acre lot located a half mile from downtown Salida.
rows.notes: Verified via WebSearch extraction of the CHA project page. Supports: 17 units, four buildings, half-acre site, Jan 1, 2026 first tenants, $1.287M Transformational Affordable Housing Grant from Colorado State Housing Board.

rows.supporting_quote: The construction and development of Jane's Place relied on a $1.3 million dollar community revitalization grant from the Colorado Office of Economic Development and International Trade (OEDIT). Jane's Place received $300,000 in government funding to install a solar array on the roof of the multigenerational housing complex. The Chaffee Housing Authority receives $350,000 a year from lodging tax monies to ensure they're solvent enough to do these projects. Chaffee County invested $260,000 from Lodging Tax into Chaffee Housing Authority's development of Jane's Place in Salida.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports all components of the brief's funding stack: $1.3M OEDIT community-revitalization, $300K solar, $350K/year ongoing lodging-tax operating support, $260K specifically from county lodging-tax reallocation for Jane's Place.

rows.supporting_quote: The two-acre parcel is owned and being developed by local restaurateur and businessman Rob Gartzman, and will result in 69 housing units – a combination of single-family homes, duplexes, townhouses, and apartment buildings intended for workforce housing. The property is located at 6907 CR 105... In April 2024 the CHA board voted to enter into a special limited partnership (SLP) with the Flour Mill project...
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports: two-acre parcel, Rob Gartzman ownership, 69-unit total, CR 105 location, CHA negotiating operating relationship (described as a special limited partnership). Brief says 'CR 105' generically; source pins to 6907 CR 105 — consistent.

rows.supporting_quote: The Flour Mill Apartments in Salida, Chaffee County received $1.1 million in Housing Development Grant funds to Chaffee Housing Authority for 20 apartments near Downtown Salida businesses, library, schools and grocery stores.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction. Supports verbatim: $1.1M Housing Development Grant, 20 apartments, awarded via Chaffee Housing Authority, December 31, 2025 announcement.

rows.supporting_quote: The Chaffee Housing Authority (CHA) is the manager for the City of Salida's Inclusionary Housing Program (IHP), with homes priced for different income levels (up to 140% Area Median Income).
rows.notes: Re-verification of s17 for active-pipeline paragraph 1; same CHA homepage cited twice. Supports 140% AMI ceiling and CHA management of the Salida IHP.

rows.supporting_quote: For a City of Salida IHP home, an interested buyer must first submit a Homebuyer Eligibility Application to the CHA who will determine eligibility to purchase a deed-restricted home.
rows.notes: WebFetch direct access denied by harness; verified via WebSearch extraction of the city Community Development inclusionary-housing page. Supports: CHA reviews homebuyer eligibility applications on the city's behalf, IHP runs up to 140% AMI.
